hero-description: InterVisions is a multidisciplinary project that combines intersectional feminist theory with participatory AI practices to address the biases and inequalities reproduced by artificial intelligence models that connect language and vision.
hero-goal: Its goal is to promote AI systems that are fairer, more inclusive, and aligned with fundamental rights.
key-aspects-intro: Explore the core concepts that guide the InterVisions project — from understanding how AI systems reproduce bias, to why an intersectional approach is essential, to the role of participation in shaping fairer technologies.
tools-intro: Four tools created by the InterVisions Project, designed to integrate an intersectional perspective into different areas of action. Each tool offers specific methodologies for analysis, evaluation, and participation.
community-intro: Share your experience and reflections with us, and help shape the project. Your input directly informs the development of our tools and methodologies.
form-thankyou-text: We've received your message and will be in touch soon.
resources-intro: A curated collection of academic papers, reports, and tools related to AI bias, intersectionality, and algorithmic justice.
tools-page-hero: Four tools created by the InterVisions Project, designed to integrate an intersectional perspective into different areas of action.
tools-page-intro: Each tool offers specific methodologies for analysis, evaluation, and participation. Together they form a comprehensive framework for intersectional AI auditing and governance.
tool1-p1: We present an intersectional framework for impact assessment, designed to be useful and adaptable to other contexts as well. Impact assessment is a systematic process that identifies, analyzes, and anticipates the potential effects of an action — such as the design, use, or governance of an AI system — on people and the environment.
tool1-p2: The proposed intersectional framework goes beyond a simple technical risk review. It emphasizes how structural inequalities — related to gender, race, class, ability, or origin, among others — can be reproduced or amplified in the creation and implementation of AI technologies.
tool1-p3: The goal is to turn impact assessment into a transformative practice that questions and modifies these dynamics, promoting fairer and more equitable outcomes.
tool2-p1: The Audit Tool is designed to assess intersectional biases in vision and language foundation models. Its goal is to make AI system auditing accessible to individuals and organizations that are usually excluded from these technical processes, with a particular focus on those most affected by AI's impacts.
tool2-p2: Its methodology combines technical analysis with participatory and intersectional approaches, making it possible to identify how biases affect people differently depending on gender, background, age, or other factors.
tool2-li1: Co-designed with diverse participants, not only technical experts
tool2-li2: Includes an intersectional analysis to capture multiple and overlapping biases
tool2-li4: Focused on real-world impacts on vulnerable communities
tool3-p1: The workshops are co-creation spaces where directly affected communities and civil society organizations actively participate in designing the project's technical tools. Their purpose is to ensure that both the participatory auditing tool and the algorithmic justice benchmark are grounded in real experiences, rather than solely in technical criteria.
tool3-p2: Through a participatory and intersectional methodology, the workshops help identify how discrimination in vision and language models affects people differently depending on their gender, background, age, or situation of vulnerability.
tool3-li1: Designed with and for affected communities
tool3-li2: Incorporate intersectionality as a methodological core, not as an add-on
tool3-li3: Directly connect civic participation with technical development
tool3-li4: Generate collective knowledge and critical capacity in relation to AI
tool4-p1: The Benchmark is a technical tool designed to assess intersectional biases in vision and language foundation models. Unlike conventional benchmarks, which only measure technical performance, this one focuses on algorithmic justice: how models affect diverse people depending on gender, background, age, or other combined identity dimensions.
tool4-p2: Its main indicators make it possible to compare model behavior in terms of fairness and bias mitigation, and to evaluate the social impact of training datasets.
tool4-li1: Assesses intersectional fairness, not just technical accuracy
tool4-li2: Built from data generated through participatory workshops
tool4-li3: Directly connected to the Audit Tool for a comprehensive evaluation
tool4-li4: Aimed at becoming an industry standard
team-page-hero: A multidisciplinary team bringing together feminist technologists, researchers, lawyers, and community organizers.
team-page-intro: InterVisions brings together researchers, technologists, and community organizers from different disciplines and institutions, united by a commitment to building fairer AI systems.
member-ec-bio:
Eva Cruells López holds a degree in Psychology and a Master's in Public and Social Policies. Her career focuses especially on the intersection of feminism, technopolitics, and digital rights, with an emphasis on combating digital violence and promoting technological sovereignty from a feminist perspective.

She has carried out research and action in the field of gender and technology, promoting technopolitical and community-based initiatives aimed at social transformation through an intersectional lens. She has worked on the design and implementation of feminist and equality public policies, as well as on addressing gender-based violence, including in digital environments.

She has held various leadership roles at the SURT Foundation, worked as a policy officer at the European Women's Lobby, and served as a consultant on equality policies. In 2006, she co-founded the cyberfeminist collective Donestech, and in 2020 she co-launched Fembloc, a feminist support service addressing digital violence. She is currently co-coordinator of the SIE on digital gender-based violence.
member-mo-bio:
A political scientist and lawyer working at the intersection of technology, law, and social justice. She contributes to the European InterVisions project through Donestech, where she promotes inclusion and equity in AI systems and develops methods to certify and document online gender-based violence and hate speech.

Previously, she led advocacy and strategic litigation at the Eticas Foundation, coordinating the Action for Algorithmic Justice coalition to address algorithmic discrimination, transparency gaps, and the systemic risks of automated decision-making. Throughout her career, she has combined legal analysis, policy design, and community-based action, with a growing focus on strategic litigation as a tool to defend civic space and human rights.
member-lg-bio:
Ramón y Cajal Research Fellow at the Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona and member of the Computer Vision Center (CVC), where he works at the intersection of computer vision, machine learning, and responsible AI. His current research explores the detection and mitigation of algorithmic bias in multimodal AI systems, and addresses questions of privacy and ethics in deep learning algorithms. He also applies computer vision techniques to the analysis and preservation of cultural heritage.

Previously, he was a TECNIOspring researcher (2018–2020), a programme co-funded by the European Commission under the Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions, and has carried out research stays at the MICC in Florence and the Intelligent Media Processing Group in Osaka. He has actively contributed to the ICDAR Robust Reading Competitions, a benchmark initiative for text understanding in images. Alongside his academic career, he has a strong interest in the social implications of AI and in making deep learning models fairer and more interpretable.
member-sl-bio:
Her work sits at the intersection of AI research, education, and creative practice: she collaborates on research projects, develops AI benchmarks and studies on dataset biases, and designs training programmes and workshops on generative AI, inclusive learning, and critical AI literacy. She creates explorations of the social impact of AI systems, educational programmes and professional training in emerging technologies, and experimental gamified projects.

With a background in mathematics, physics, and AI, she combines technical rigour with participatory design and creative experimentation. Her goal is to foster agency, critical thinking, and meaningful social impact, accompanying people to engage with AI in an active and reflective way.
member-sr-bio:
Sonia Ruiz García is a political scientist with a PhD in Political Sciences from the Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona, with a thesis focused on work, gender, and the feminist movement. She is currently coordinator of the Area of Feminisms and Equality at the Diputació de Barcelona.

Throughout her career she has combined institutional practice, academic research, and feminist activism: she was Policy Officer at the European Women's Lobby in Brussels, director of the Equality Plan at the Diputació de Barcelona, adviser in the Cabinet of the Minister of Defence, and postdoctoral researcher at the Centre for Diversity Policy Research and Practice at Oxford Brookes University. She has also directed the Gender and Time Policy Services at the Barcelona City Council. Her research focuses on feminism, public policies, social movements, and platform economies, and she has published on gender impact assessment in fiscal policies and on work in digital environments.
member-ae-bio: Advisory Board member contributing expertise on gender equality and digital rights.
member-sa-bio: Director of Presidency Services at the Barcelona Provincial Council. Passionate about data and disruptive technologies as a driver for generating public value and improving public policies and services.
member-mm-bio: Her professional career initially focused on accounting and auditing services in the private sector. Later, she spent 10 years working in the central budgetary services of the Barcelona Provincial Council, and for the past 4 years she has been part of the Equality Policies Service, where she has led the Comparative Circle of Gender Equality Services project, as well as other areas related to data management, results analysis, and public awareness programs on gender equality.
member-ap-bio: Urban psychologist working at the intersection of social design, community engagement, and spatial transformation processes. She developed her career specializing in inclusive urbanism.
member-nv-bio: Professor of the Department of Sociology and Director of the Equality Unit at the University of Barcelona. Co-founder of Donestech and Fembloc collective. Member of various feminist organizations and for social transformation. She was Director General in the first Department of Equality and Feminism of the Generalitat de Catalunya.
member-dk-bio: Dimosthenis Karatzas is a full professor at the Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona, associate director of the Computer Vision Center and ICREA academia professor. He leads the research line on vision, language and reading. His research focuses on multimodal learning for document understanding.
member-ya-bio: Yuki leads the Fundamental AI Lab at the University of Technology in Nuremberg. Prior to this he was an assistant professor at the University of Amsterdam and a PhD at VGG at the University of Oxford. His research focuses on multimodal and self-supervised learning.
member-ht-bio: H. Titilola Olojede is an academic whose research intersects ethics of AI and gender. She represents Society of Gender Professionals with InterVisions.
member-gp-bio: Dr Piga is an international specialist in gender equality and social inclusion with over 25 years' experience across policy, fieldwork, advisory roles and academic research. She serves on the board and contributes her expertise on gender stereotypes and intersectional biases in data, algorithms, and decision-making systems.
member-ac-bio: Cyberfeminist, researcher, and founder of Donestech (2006) and Fembloc (2022), exploring gender-technology intersections and supporting survivors of Technology Facilitated Gender-Based Violence. For a decade, she coordinated international digital security programs for Women Human Rights Defenders at Tactical Tech and for Rapid Responders communities at Digital Defenders.
member-lm-bio: Biotechnologist and specialist in science communication and education at Computer Vision Center (CVC), focusing on the intersection of AI, social impact and open innovation. She works across co-creation processes, Living Labs and inclusion-driven initiatives, and is an active member of CVC's Equality, Diversity and Inclusion Committee.
member-mp-bio: A computer programmer at the Dabne cooperative, she works at the intersection of the technical and the social, investigating, from a gender perspective, how technologies and power relations intertwine. She is the author of the book "Artificial Intelligence: To Play or to Break the Deck."
member-mc-bio: PhD in Political Science. She is currently a senior researcher at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center in the area of science, citizenship and policies. Her career combines academic research (in the field of social and gender inequalities) with institutional consultancy in areas such as equality, social rights and scientific and academic policy.
member-ma-bio: Maya Aidlin-Perlman is a research fellow funded by Yale University, whose work lies at the intersection of gender economics, artificial intelligence, and public policy. Her research examines how AI systems have gender-differentiated impacts in the labor market. She holds a double degree in Economics and International Relations from Yale University, where she also worked as a research assistant in gender economics and wrote her undergraduate thesis on digital training programs for women.
member-nn-bio: Nadia is a researcher and technologist working in civic tech and AI governance, designing processes and frameworks for democratic and responsible technology.
member-am-bio: Adri received his B.S. degree in Data Engineering from Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona in 2022. He received his M.S. degree in 2023 and is currently a Ph.D. Candidate in the Document Analysis Group at the Centre de Visió per Computador. His research interests are in the areas of cultural heritage, graph analysis, information retrieval, and data governance.
member-gf-bio: Participation coordinator at InterVisions and freelance consultant in participation, democratic governance of AI and human rights. Currently working on the transfer of academic knowledge to policymakers for different research projects.
member-ab-bio: Coordinator of participation in InterVisions and member of Radical Data, working on strategic and creative projects at the intersection of art, technology and social innovation.
privacy-page-text: This page will contain the privacy policy for the InterVisions project. Content to be added.
